elements.sent-2411-001: This study investigates the site occupancy preferences of Al in Ta(Fe$_{1-x}$Al$_x$)$_2$ Laves phases using first-principles calculations, with Al concentrations ranging from 0 at.\% to 50 at.\%.
elements.sent-2411-002: Al atoms exhibit a strong preference for occupying the $2a$ Wyckoff sites, with higher concentrations leading to more stable configurations when these sites are fully occupied.
elements.sent-2411-003: Magnetic configurations were explored, revealing that anti-ferromagnetic ordering is the most favorable at ground states.
elements.sent-2411-004: A phase diagram based on the chemical potential of Al was constructed to map phase stability, where Ta$_4$Fe$_6$Al$_2$ and Ta$_4$Fe$_2$Al$_6$ exhibit the largest stability ranges.
elements.sent-2411-005: The correlation between lattice distortions and phase stability was examined, demonstrating that symmetric Al distributions contribute to greater stability.
elements.sent-2411-006: These findings offer insight into the structural motifs expected in the Ta-Fe-Al system, providing a foundation for future investigations of structure-property correlations.
elements.sent-2411-007: Tuning the mechanical properties of Laves phases enables the development of materials capable of withstanding extreme temperatures, offering high oxidation resistance, and maintaining low density or improved processability for commercial applications \cite{livingston1992laves,stein2021laves,xu2021tailoring,sui2021laves .}
elements.sent-2411-008: Laves phases, especially in ternary compositions, hold significant potential for advancing high-entropy alloys (HEAs) given their prevalence as intermetallic phases in these materials \cite{tsai2019intermetallic .}
elements.sent-2411-009: Beyond structural applications, Laves phases are increasingly used in hydrogen storage, superconductors, and wear- and corrosion-resistant materials; rare-earth-containing Laves phases are also noted for their magnetic applications \cite{stein2021laves, yartys2022laves .}
elements.sent-2411-010: However, their inherent brittleness at room temperature—attributed to the dense atomic packing of large (A) and small (B) atoms in AB$_2$ crystal structures—presents a challenge for structural applications \cite{laves1934kristallstruktur,laves1935kristallstruktur .}
elements.sent-2411-011: While plasticity is crucial for structural material applications, understanding the elastic properties, their anisotropy, and their dependence on composition is equally important for designing materials with both functional and structural integrity.
elements.sent-2411-012: Enhanced knowledge of the mechanical behavior of Laves phases is therefore essential for advancing complex alloy systems and accessing superior functional properties.
elements.sent-2411-013: The conventional unit cell of the C14 Laves phase (space group $P6_{3 /mmc$ and prototype MgZn$_2$) consists of a hexagonal cell of 12 atoms, composed of alternating stacks of triple layers and Kagomé layers along the $\langle c \rangle$ axis.}
elements.sent-2411-014: The A atoms occupy the Wyckoff site $4f$ with a coordination number of 16 (Z=16), sandwiching the smaller B atoms at the Wyckoff positions of $2a$ within the triple layers when viewed along the $\langle a \rangle$ direction.
elements.sent-2411-015: B atoms in the Kagomé layers are located at the Wyckoff position $6h$ and exhibit icosahedral coordination (Z=12).
elements.sent-2411-016: The B atoms form a sublattice of tetrahedra along the vertices of the $\langle c \rangle$ axis.
elements.sent-2411-017: These tetrahedra alternate between face-sharing and vertex-sharing, creating trigonal bipyramids joined at their apexes (Figure \ref{fig:1 a).}
elements.sent-2411-018: These chains are interconnected in the basal plane, forming large polyhedra with the A atoms situated at the centers of the polyhedra.
elements.sent-2411-019: Half of the triangular B-atom nets in the Kagomé layer are "capped" by a B-atom in the $2a$ position, while the other half remain "uncapped" when viewed along the $\langle c \rangle$ axis (see Figure \ref{fig:1 b).}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.75\linewidth]{fig_lavesphase_crys_ZX_NU_v03.png} \caption{The conventional unit cell of C14 TaFe$_2$ viewed from two perspectives: (a) an off-axis view and (b) a view along the $\langle c \rangle$ axis. The structure consists of alternating stacks of a triple layer and a Kagomé layer along the $\langle c \rangle$ direction. The triple layer consists of the $4f$ Ta atoms (brown) that sandwich the $2a$ Fe atoms (light blue). The Kagomé Fe layers, shown in two different shades of dark blue, are offset, as the atoms in the upper and lower layers do not align on top of one another.} \label{fig:1}
elements.sent-2411-020: Chemical composition has long been investigated as a means to adjust the mechanical properties of Laves phases.\cite{takasugi1996deformability,thoma1997elastic,yoshida1997alloying,chen1998factors,takata2016nanoindentation,FREUND2024120124 and their composites \cite{yurchenko2016effect,zubair2019role,zubair2023laves,han2023effects .}}
elements.sent-2411-021: Introducing an additional alloying element to the Laves phases can lead to either hardening or softening, depending on the alloying system \cite{takasugi1996deformability,thoma1997elastic,yoshida1997alloying,chen1998factors,takata2016nanoindentation,FREUND2024120124 .}
elements.sent-2411-022: Much of these efforts rely on trial and error, as a fundamental understanding of the mechanical response, particularly in terms of dislocation mechanisms under various stimuli, remains lacking.
elements.sent-2411-023: To optimize the mechanical properties of Laves phases by modulating composition, it is essential to consider that any Laves phase with a composition away from a pure AB$_2$ composition may possess an atomic structure which differs from the ideal stoichiometric Laves phases in terms of the formation of point defects, such as anti-site atoms on either sublattice, structural vacancies, and ordered or disordered integration of ternary elements.
elements.sent-2411-024: It is therefore imperative to first understand the site occupancy preferences of alloying elements in any Laves phase of interest.
elements.sent-2411-025: The site occupancy preferences in ternary C14 Laves phases have been investigated using X-ray diffraction and ab-initio calculations.
elements.sent-2411-026: At the stoichiometric 1/3 A-composition, it is widely agreed upon that the ternary element occupies either the $2a$ or $6h$ B sites \cite{majzoub2003rietveld,kerkau2009site,yan2009laves,yamagata2023site , even when its atomic radius is close to that of the A-atom \cite{yamagata2023site .}}
elements.sent-2411-027: The preference for the $6h$ or the $2a$ B-sites tends to vary depending on the material system.
elements.sent-2411-028: Some systems \cite{grytsiv2006atom,yan2008ternary,kerkau2009site show distinct composition-dependent site occupancy preferences, while in others, the occupation of the $2a$ and $6h$ sites is considered random \cite{majzoub2003rietveld,yan2009laves,yan2022site,yamagata2023site .}}
elements.sent-2411-029: The stability of the ternary Laves phase has been discussed in terms of the geometric factors, such as the lattice parameters ($c/a$) or atomic radii (r$_B$/r$_A$) ratios \cite{yamagata2023site .}
elements.sent-2411-030: However, the qualitative differences in the progression of the $c/a$ ratio with composition across stoichiometric and off-stoichiometric Laves phases suggest that these simple geometric factors alone are insufficient to predict stability.
elements.sent-2411-031: For instance, variations in site occupation trends observed in Zr(V$_{1-x}$Co$_x$)$_2$ and Nb(Cr$_{1-x}$Co$_x$)$_2$ \cite{kerkau2009site indicate that bonding effects play a significant role in determining site occupancy, making each ternary Laves phase system potentially unique.}
elements.sent-2411-032: In this work, the site occupancy of a common Laves phase that possesses a large stability range, namely the C14 Ta(Fe,Al)$_2$, was investigated.
elements.sent-2411-033: It has been identified as one of the less brittle Laves precipitates that can form in the $\alpha$-Fe matrix \cite{jones1972laves .}
elements.sent-2411-034: The Fe-Ta-Al phase diagram has been explored extensively by Witusiewicz et al. \cite{witusiewicz2013experimental and elaborated further by Raghavan et al. \cite{raghavan2013phase , showing that the homogeneous ternary Ta-Fe-Al C14 Laves phase constitutes a wide range of Fe and Al concentrations, but a narrow range of Ta concentrations.}}
elements.sent-2411-035: As the solubility of Al in the Ta-Fe-Al Laves phase can reach up to 52 at.$\%$ \cite{witusiewicz2013experimental , the mechanical properties of the TaFe$_2$ phase could be tunable through Al substitution.}
elements.sent-2411-036: The site occupancy preferences of Al in Ta(Fe$_{1-x}$Al$_x$)$_2$ Laves phases, with Al concentrations ranging from 8.3 at.$\%$ to 50 at.$\%$, were explored using density functional theory (DFT).
elements.sent-2411-037: The stable magnetic configurations of each Ta(Fe$_{1-x}$Al$_x$)$_2$ composition were identified.
elements.sent-2411-038: A phase diagram of the Ta(Fe$_{1-x}$Al$_x$)$_2$ C14 Laves phases as a function of the Al chemical potential was constructed, informed by experimental phase boundaries.
elements.sent-2411-039: Correlations between lattice distortion and phase stability were also examined.

elements.sent-2411-040: DFT calculations were performed using the Vienna Ab Initio Simulation Package (VASP) \cite{kresse1996efficient,kresse1993ab .}
elements.sent-2411-041: The Projector Augmented Wave (PAW) potential was employed to construct the planewave basis set \cite{kresse1999ultrasoft , with the exchange-correlation functional using the generalized gradient approximation (GGA) of Perdew–Burke–Ernzerhof (PBE) \cite{perdew1996generalized .}}
elements.sent-2411-042: The kinetic energy cutoff for the planewave basis set was set to 550 eV.
elements.sent-2411-043: These basis sets implement frozen core electrons, as bonding is assumed to occur only among valence electrons.
elements.sent-2411-044: In the PAW potentials used, Fe $4s^1, 3d^7$, Ta $6s^1, 5d^4, 5p^6$ and Al $3p^1, 3s^2$ were set as the valence orbitals.
elements.sent-2411-045: In each geometry optimization calculation, the Fermi level was smeared using the 1$^{st}$ order Methfessel-Paxton method with a $\sigma$ level of 0.1 eV \cite{methfessel1989high .}
elements.sent-2411-046: Spin polarization was enabled in all calculations.
elements.sent-2411-047: The self-consistent field (SCF) cycles and geometric optimization were considered converged when the energy difference reached 10$^{-6}$ eV and the forces were less than 0.02 eV/$\mathrm{\AA{ }$.}
elements.sent-2411-048: The Brillouin zone was sampled using the Monkhorst-Pack k-point scheme \cite{monkhorst1976special .}
elements.sent-2411-049: Geometry optimizations of the conventional unit cell were performed using a gamma-centered $k$-point grid of 10 $\times$ 10 $\times$ 5.
elements.sent-2411-050: VESTA \cite{momma2011vesta and OVITO \cite{stukowski2009visualization were used for visualization.}}
elements.sent-2411-051: The C14 Laves phase, possessing a hexagonal crystal structure, features independent lattice parameters $a$ and $c$, and the angles $\alpha, \beta,$ and $\gamma$ are fixed at 90$^{\circ}$, 90$^{\circ}$, and 120$^{\circ}$, respectively, in its conventional unit cell.
elements.sent-2411-052: The ground-state lattice parameters were achieved by fully relaxing the atomic position, cell shape, and cell size.
elements.sent-2411-053: Magnetism was included in the configurational sampling by permuting the spin-up and spin-down arrangement across all Fe atoms, with each configuration considered as a separate structure.

elements.figure-2411-005: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{fig_pp_phasediagn_v06.png} \caption{Phase diagram of Ta(Fe$_{1-x}$Al$_x$)$_2$ as a function of Al chemical potential, referenced to the bulk energy of Al. elements.figure-2411-006: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{fig_bonddistort_v06.png} \caption{Descriptors of lattice distortion within the C14 Laves phase structure. Schematics show (a) Kagomé layer distortion and (b) interlayer distortion along the $\langle c \rangle$ axis. The first distortion parameter is the ratio of B-B atom distances in the small capped triangles ($d_{6h-6h}$) and uncapped triangles ($d'_{6h-6h}$). The second parameter is the distortion of the trigonal pyramidal bonds between the Kagomé B atoms (at position $6h$) and the triple-layer B atom (at position $2a$), measured as the ratio of the maximum to the minimum $d_{6h-2a}$. The third parameter is based on the interplanar distance between the Kagomé layer and the $2a$ atom of the triple layer. The ratio is taken between the interplanar distances of the upper ($z_{2a-6h}$) and the lower layers ($z'_{2a-6h}$). The corresponding distortion parameters for each composition are respectively shown in (c) i, ii and iii.} \label{fig:distort}

elements.sent-2411-099: The large region of stability is obtained because of the assumption that an extremely low concentration of Al (1 at.\% Al) can be achieved, where solute-solute Al interactions are limited.

In this context, each composition is regarded as a potential structural motif within a distribution of stable and metastable configurations above the ground state.}
elements.sent-2411-101: The 1D phase diagram illustrates that certain structural motifs exhibit greater stability within specific chemical potential windows.
elements.sent-2411-102: Notably, only the Ta$_4$Fe$_6$Al$_2$ and Ta$_4$Fe$_2$Al$_6$ compositions have relatively large windows of stability, while other ternary compositions, such as Ta$_4$Fe$_7$Al$_1$, remain metastable throughout the Al chemical potential range from -6.7 to -0.3 eV.
elements.sent-2411-103: The metastability of the Ta$_{32}$Fe$_{63}$Al$_1$ motif relative to TaFe$_2$ in the low Al chemical potential range suggests that Al atoms prefer to form an ordered superstructure rather than a random distribution.
elements.sent-2411-104: Within the chemical potential range from -1.1 to -0.6 eV, Ta$_4$Fe$_6$Al$_2$ with full Al substitution at the $2a$-sites and anti-ferromagnetic ordering is the dominant structural motif.
elements.sent-2411-105: Above -0.6 eV, the Ta$_4$Fe$_2$Al$_6$ configuration with anti-ferromagnetic ordering becomes the most stable.
elements.sent-2411-106: The metastability of other configurations at the low Al chemical potential range suggests that Al occupancy in the Kagomé layers is less favorable when the Al concentration is low.
elements.sent-2411-107: Once the $2a$ sites are saturated by Al, further substitution at the $6h$ sites (in the Kagomé layer) requires a significantly higher chemical potential to act as the driving force.
elements.sent-2411-108: A narrow chemical potential window around -0.6 eV was obtained, where Ta$_4$Fe$_3$Al$_5$ becomes the most stable structure, though with only minor energy differences compared to other configurations.
elements.sent-2411-109: The intersection of formation energy lines for the metastable states near -0.6 eV implies that free energy contributions, e.g. through thermal and entropy changes, may increase the prominence of other intermediate configurations within this chemical potential range.

elements.sent-2411-149: In this work, the site occupancy preferences and magnetic configurations of Ta(Fe$_{1-x}$Al$_x$)$_2$ C14 Laves phases across a wide range of Al concentrations were investigated using DFT.
elements.sent-2411-150: The key outcomes are as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-151: Al shows a strong preference for occupying the $2a$ sites across all Ta(Fe$_{1-x}$Al$_x$)$_2$ compositions.
elements.sent-2411-152: Full Al substitution at the $2a$ sites is energetically favorable, while configurations with partial occupancy at the $2a$ sites or Al substitution at the $6h$ sites result in higher energy configurations.
elements.sent-2411-153: The distribution of Al between adjacent Kagomé layers is symmetric in the lowest-energy configurations.
elements.sent-2411-154: Even Al distribution is energetically favorable, while configurations with one fully substituted Kagomé layer and the other unsubstituted, are the least stable.
elements.sent-2411-155: A phase diagram was constructed as a function of Al chemical potential, revealing that Fe$_6$Al$_2$Ta$_4$ and Fe$_2$Al$_6$Ta$_4$ exhibit the largest stability ranges, while other compositions remain metastable, exhibiting narrow or no stability windows.
elements.sent-2411-156: Anti-ferromagnetic ordering is generally the most stable magnetic configuration across all compositions.
elements.sent-2411-157: Although magnetic fluctuations and degenerate configurations suggest complex magnetic behavior, the influence of magnetism on phase stability is relatively minor compared to site occupancy.
elements.sent-2411-158: Structural stability correlates with lattice distortion, with symmetry in the interlayer distances ($z_{2a-6h /z^{\prime _{2a-6h}$) emerging as a strong geometric descriptor, with minimal distortion correlating with stable configurations in even-numbered Al compositions.}}
elements.sent-2411-159: The stable compositions identified theoretically align with experimentally synthesized Ta-Fe-Al Laves phases, though minor deviations in composition suggest heterogeneity in the chemical distribution or free energy contributions influencing phase stability.
